flashcards.q: Which terminal of an electric cell is the small protruding metal cap?
flashcards.a: The positive (+ve) terminal.

flashcards.q: Which terminal of an electric cell is the flat metal disc?
flashcards.a: The negative (−ve) terminal.

flashcards.q: In a battery, how is the positive terminal of one cell connected to the next?
flashcards.a: The positive terminal of one cell is connected to the negative terminal of the next cell (series connection).

flashcards.q: What does connecting more cells in a battery provide?
flashcards.a: More energy and/or power to the circuit for a longer duration.

flashcards.q: True or False: The term 'battery' can be used even for a single cell.
flashcards.a: True — e.g., the single cell in a mobile phone is commonly called a battery.

flashcards.q: What is the glowing thin wire inside an incandescent lamp called?
flashcards.a: The filament.

flashcards.q: Does an incandescent lamp require a specific polarity when connected in a circuit?
flashcards.a: No — an incandescent lamp works regardless of which terminal connects to positive or negative.

flashcards.q: What is a fused lamp?
flashcards.a: A lamp with a broken filament — the break stops current flow, preventing the lamp from glowing.

flashcards.q: Does an LED have a filament?
flashcards.a: No. LEDs do not have filaments; they produce light electronically.

flashcards.q: Which wire of an LED is its positive terminal?
flashcards.a: The longer wire is the positive (+ve) terminal.

flashcards.q: Which wire of an LED is its negative terminal?
flashcards.a: The shorter wire is the negative (−ve) terminal.

flashcards.q: In which direction can current flow through an LED?
flashcards.a: In only one direction — from its positive terminal to its negative terminal.

flashcards.q: What happens if an LED is connected with reversed polarity?
flashcards.a: The LED does not glow — current cannot flow through it in the reverse direction.

flashcards.q: What is the conventional direction of electric current in a closed circuit?
flashcards.a: From the positive terminal to the negative terminal of the electric cell (through the external circuit).

flashcards.q: What is an open circuit?
flashcards.a: A circuit with a break or gap — current cannot flow and the lamp does not glow.

flashcards.q: What is a closed circuit?
flashcards.a: A complete, unbroken circuit — current flows and the lamp glows.

flashcards.q: Where in a circuit can a switch be placed?
flashcards.a: Anywhere in the circuit — a switch at any point controls the entire circuit.

flashcards.q: In the OFF position of a switch, what prevents current from flowing?
flashcards.a: An air gap between the switch contacts — the gap breaks the circuit.

flashcards.q: In a circuit diagram, which line of the cell symbol is longer?
flashcards.a: The longer line represents the positive (+ve) terminal.

flashcards.q: In a circuit diagram, which line of the cell symbol is shorter?
flashcards.a: The shorter (thick) line represents the negative (−ve) terminal.

flashcards.q: In the LED symbol in a circuit diagram, what do the two arrows indicate?
flashcards.a: That light is emitted by the LED.

flashcards.q: In the LED symbol, what does the direction of the triangle indicate?
flashcards.a: The direction in which current can flow through the LED.

flashcards.q: Name the full forms of IEC, ANSI, and IEEE.
flashcards.a: IEC: International Electrotechnical Commission; ANSI: American National Standards Institute; IEEE: Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers.

flashcards.q: Why are standard circuit symbols important?
flashcards.a: They allow people from different countries and industries to understand each other's circuit diagrams easily.

flashcards.q: Which three metals are the best electrical conductors (in order)?
flashcards.a: Silver (best), gold, and copper.

flashcards.q: Why is copper preferred over silver for making electrical wires?
flashcards.a: Copper has a comparatively lower cost and is more abundantly available.

flashcards.q: Is pencil lead (graphite) a conductor or insulator?
flashcards.a: Conductor.

flashcards.q: Name three materials used as electrical insulators.
flashcards.a: Plastic, rubber, and ceramics (any three: also glass, wood, paper, wax, cork).

flashcards.q: Why are electric wires covered with plastic or rubber?
flashcards.a: To prevent electric shocks and short circuits — plastic and rubber are insulators that stop current from escaping the wire.

flashcards.q: Is the human body a conductor or insulator of electricity?
flashcards.a: Conductor — which is why electric current passing through the body can cause injury or death.

flashcards.q: Why is it dangerous to touch electrical switches with wet hands?
flashcards.a: Water lowers the skin's electrical resistance, allowing more current to pass through the body, greatly increasing the risk of electric shock.

flashcards.q: For experiments with electricity, which type of source should students use?
flashcards.a: Only cells or batteries (e.g., from torchlights, clocks, remotes) — never mains electricity or generators.

flashcards.q: What type of current do cells and batteries produce?
flashcards.a: Direct Current (DC) — flows in one direction only.

flashcards.q: What type of current comes from power plants through wall sockets?
flashcards.a: Alternating Current (AC) — periodically reverses direction.

flashcards.q: Name three devices powered by cylindrical batteries.
flashcards.a: Torchlights, wall clocks, remote controls (also: toys, radios).

flashcards.q: Name two devices that use button cells.
flashcards.a: Watches and hearing aids.

flashcards.q: Name two devices that use rechargeable batteries.
flashcards.a: Mobile phones and laptops (also: electric vehicles).

flashcards.q: What was the source of electricity at the Bhakra Nangal Dam mentioned in the chapter?
flashcards.a: Hydroelectric power — the force of falling water is used to generate electricity.

flashcards.q: Name four sources of electricity generation mentioned in the chapter.
flashcards.a: Windmills (wind energy), solar panels (solar energy), falling water (hydroelectric), and burning natural gas or coal (thermal).

flashcards.q: True or False: Insulators are not useful in electrical systems.
flashcards.a: False. Insulators are essential — they cover conductors to prevent electric shocks, short circuits, and accidental current flow.

flashcards.q: What is the purpose of stripping about 1 cm of plastic from wire ends before making a circuit?
flashcards.a: To expose the metal conductor beneath — without stripping, the insulating plastic prevents current from crossing the connection point.

flashcards.q: If a metal strip inside a battery compartment connects two cells, what terminals does it join?
flashcards.a: The positive terminal of one cell to the negative terminal of the next cell.

flashcards.q: Can a switch be used to control an LED circuit?
flashcards.a: Yes — a switch can be placed anywhere in any electrical circuit, including one with an LED, to turn it on or off.

flashcards.q: Name two uses of conductors and two uses of insulators in electrical equipment.
flashcards.a: Conductors: electrical wires, switch contacts, plug pins, connectors. Insulators: wire coverings, plug bodies, switch casings, ceramic holders.
